q: The human eye consists of sense organs which are capable of receiving visual images and sending the information to the brain.
The eyelid functions primarily to control the amount of light entering the eye.
The cornea is a tough, transparent membrane covering the back portion of the eye.
Aqueous humour fills the region between the eyelens and the retina.
The iris is the colored portion of the eye and controls the amount of light energy entering the eye.
The pupil is located on the outer edge of the iris, allowing light to pass through.
The pupil's function is similar to the shutter in a camera.
In bright light, the pupil automatically dilates.
The ciliary muscles are attached to the cornea and adjust its curvature.
The ciliary muscles can vary the thickness of the eyelens, thus alter the focal length of the lens to obtain a sharp image on the retina.
When viewing near objects, ciliary muscles relax, making the eyelens less convex.
When viewing distant objects, ciliary muscles contract, making the eyelens more convex.
The retina is a light-insensitive area at the front of the eye.
The retina consists of rods and cones.
The yellow spot on the retina contains a high concentration of rods exclusively.
There is a spot on the retina which has no rods or cones.
When an image is formed at the blind spot, it cannot be seen at all.
The light energy falling on the retina is transmitted to the brain by means of the optic nerve.
The cornea, the aqueous humour, the eyelens and the vitreous humour are all non-refracting substances.
The image of an object formed on the retina by these four substances is virtual, erect and larger than the size of the object.
Farsightedness is also known as myopia.
In farsightedness, distant objects are blurry, while nearby objects are seen clearly.
For a person with farsightedness, the rays from near objects intersect at a point in front of the retina after passing through the eyelens.
Farsightedness can be caused by an eyeball which is too long.
Farsightedness can be corrected by the use of spectacles with diverging lens.
Nearsightedness is a defect of vision in which near objects are seen clearly, but objects far away are blurry.
For a person with nearsightedness, the rays from distant objects intersect at a point behind the retina after passing through the eyelens.
Nearsightedness can be caused by an eyeball which is too short.
Nearsightedness can be corrected by the use of spectacles with converging lens.
The common defects of vision, farsightedness and nearsightedness, are caused by the ciliary muscles which cannot contract or relax sufficiently or by wrongly shaped eyeballs.
The least distance of distinct vision for people with normal vision is about 25 cm.
People with normal vision can only see objects clearly up to a maximum distance of 25 cm.
When objects at infinity are viewed, the eyes are strained and cannot be viewed conveniently for a long time.
The iris adjusts the focal length of the eye's lens to obtain a sharp image on the retina.
